metadata.subject: English Language Arts
metadata.description: Spoken Language 12 is the capstone oral communication course, developing students as sophisticated, versatile, and reflective communicators. Students engage with complex oral texts, master advanced rhetorical and performative techniques, and explore the relationship between oral language, power, and social change. Indigenous oral traditions are honoured as primary knowledge and artistic systems. Students leave the course with a refined personal voice and the oral communication competencies needed for post-secondary, professional, and civic life.
metadata.general_description: The capstone oral communication course developing sophisticated rhetoric, performance, critical listening, and advocacy — preparing students for effective communication in post-secondary, professional, and civic life.

big_ideas.statement: Spoken language is a primary vehicle for social change, advocacy, and justice.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students examine how oral communication has driven social movements, political change, and community transformation — and develop their own capacity to use speech as a force for positive change.

big_ideas.statement: Masterful speakers understand and respond to the full complexity of rhetorical situations.
big_ideas.elaboration: Graduating students analyze and produce sophisticated oral texts that demonstrate command of rhetorical purpose, audience awareness, cultural context, and stylistic choice.

big_ideas.statement: Oral traditions carry the values, knowledge, and identity of communities across generations.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students engage at depth with Indigenous and global oral traditions — understanding their sophistication, purpose, and the responsibility that comes with engaging with others' oral heritage.

big_ideas.statement: Reflective communicators continuously develop their craft through practice, feedback, and self-awareness.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students apply metacognitive awareness to their own speaking development, articulating their growth, strengths, and ongoing goals as communicators.

competencies.raw: Analyze and evaluate the craft, structure, and impact of complex oral texts; synthesize perspectives from diverse oral traditions; critically evaluate the relationship between spoken language, power, and identity.
i_can_statements: I can analyze and evaluate the craft, structure, and rhetorical effectiveness of complex speeches, performances, and oral texts.
I can synthesize insights from diverse oral traditions — including Indigenous, political, artistic, and community voices.
I can critically examine how spoken language reflects, constructs, and challenges power dynamics and social norms.
I can connect oral communication to questions of identity, justice, and civic responsibility.

competencies.raw: Create and deliver sophisticated oral texts demonstrating mastery of rhetorical craft and personal voice; lead and facilitate complex oral communication contexts; reflect critically on personal growth as a communicator.
i_can_statements: I can create and deliver polished, purposeful oral presentations and performances that demonstrate rhetorical mastery.
I can lead and facilitate complex oral communication contexts — debates, seminars, community forums.
I can use spoken language as a tool for advocacy and social change.
I can reflect critically and specifically on my growth as a communicator and articulate clear ongoing goals.
I can mentor peers in developing their oral communication skills.

areas.name: Advanced Rhetoric and Advocacy
areas.raw: Sophisticated rhetorical analysis and advocacy-focused public address.
topics: Rhetorical analysis at depth: kairos, rhetorical situation, and the full range of appeals
Advocacy speech: crafting and delivering speeches for social change and community action
Political and civic oratory: analysing and producing speeches in democratic and community contexts
TED-style talks: blending expertise, narrative, and persuasion for broad audiences
Crisis communication: speaking effectively in high-stakes, emotionally charged situations
Rhetorical legacy: examining how landmark speeches shaped history and public consciousness

areas.name: Advanced Storytelling and Oral Tradition
areas.raw: Mastery of oral narrative and deep engagement with oral traditions.
topics: Advanced personal narrative: vulnerability, authenticity, and craft in personal storytelling
Oral history methodology: ethical frameworks for collecting, interpreting, and sharing community stories
Indigenous protocol and reciprocity: responsibilities when engaging with First Peoples oral traditions
Comparative oral traditions: examining oral narrative structures and purposes across world cultures
Story as advocacy: using narrative for social justice and community healing
Digital oral preservation: podcasting, oral archives, and community storytelling platforms

areas.name: Facilitating Dialogue and Deliberation
areas.raw: Leading complex dialogue, deliberative processes, and community communication.
topics: Facilitating complex discussions: managing diverse viewpoints, ensuring inclusion, and building consensus
Restorative circles: Indigenous and community-based approaches to dialogue and conflict resolution
Deliberative democracy: structured community dialogue on public issues
Navigating difficult conversations: race, privilege, trauma, and power in oral contexts
Mediation skills: principles and practice of oral conflict resolution
Community forum design and facilitation

areas.name: Performance and Spoken Word Mastery
areas.raw: Advanced performance, spoken word artistry, and oral artistic expression.
topics: Spoken word as social commentary: performance poetry and political voice
Full performance piece: devising, rehearsing, and delivering a polished solo or ensemble spoken word work
Oral interpretation of complex texts: philosophical, literary, and cross-cultural material
Voice and body mastery: breath, resonance, physicality, and spatial awareness in performance
Collaborative oral performance: ensemble pieces, choral work, and community storytelling
Curating and presenting an oral performance portfolio

areas.name: Critical Listening and Communication Ethics
areas.raw: Advanced critical listening, media literacy, and ethical oral communication.
topics: Evaluating oral media: podcasts, speeches, interviews — credibility, bias, and rhetorical purpose
Propaganda and manipulation: identifying deceptive oral rhetoric in political and commercial contexts
Ethical communication: honesty, consent, representation, and cultural respect in speaking
Listening as leadership: how empathetic listening builds trust, community, and effective teams
Intercultural communication: navigating differences in communication styles and norms respectfully
Developing a personal communication ethics statement
